---

We’ve mentioned financial covenants several times in this module and I’ve covered them at
a very high level.
Covenant essentially means contract or deal. They’re conditions that will be included in the
loan agreement that you agree to follow at the lender’s request.
There are a few different types you might encounter:
1. 	Affirmative Loan Covenants
2. 	Financial Loan Covenants
3. 	Negative Loan Covenants
Affirmative loan covenants typically require you to do something, such as purchase adequate
insurance, maintain the underlying assets or collateral, or deliver audit or financial
statements.
Financial loan covenants usually ask you to maintain something. They’ll set benchmarks,
metrics, or ratios that act as the vital signs of the business to measure profitability and cash
flow performance.
Since the lenders aren’t on site at the business, these indicators keep them informed of how
the business is doing so they know whether it's on track or not.
And then negative loan covenants act as restrictions. These create boundaries that restrict
you from doing certain things, like paying out dividends, selling assets, or taking out of the
loans.
You will find these covenants in every loan agreement you do in some form.
Since the lender isn’t there, they need to be able to monitor the business and manage it
from a distance. So they’ll ask you to report on these covenant items periodically.
These covenants are usually negotiable to an extent. And some specific covenants aren’t
always requirements, meaning you can reject them as part of the agreement.
Let’s dive deeper into each covenant.
Financial Covenants 101
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 5 --

Affirmative Loan Covenants
An affirmative loan covenant requires the borrower to perform certain actions and meet
specific benchmarks.
Like all of these covenants, it acts as investor protection. If there’s any kind of issue then
the covenant will call for some kind of remediation for the lender.
And the covenant may outline some kind of grave period in case of default where the
borrower can fix the violation. However, if it isn’t resolved the loan then immediately
defaults and the borrower must pay the full principal and any accrued interest.
The requirements in the covenant might be as basic as keeping proper accounting,
maintaining some kind of credit rating, or complying with regulation or laws… Things that
the borrower should already be striving to do.
The investor just wants reassurance and contractual requirements in place to protect them
in case that becomes an issue.
Some other requirements might be requiring that the business maintains some adequate
level of insurance.
Some covenants will be very specific and others will be quite broad. So you’ll want to read
everything very carefully before you sign.
Financial Loan Covenants
Financial loan covenants require you to maintain certain things.
One common one is liquidity. They’ll ask that you calculate a liquidity ratio on a regular
basis and they might require that ratio be above a certain threshold.
For the lender, having liquidity in their investment improves the likelihood that they’ll be
paid back for the loan.
Another metric that they’ll look at is debt-to-equity ratio… Which compares liabilities to
shareholder equity. It can be used to evaluate how much leverage a company is using.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 5 --

A higher debt-to-debt ratio usually indicates a company that is a higher risk to lenders. So
they’ll require you to have a certain ratio there as well.
Another typical financial loan covenant is maintaining certain working capital minimums.
They might also look at what your credit balance is versus the amount in receivables you
have ready to be paid.
Lenders will use their preferred metrics to measure the business and confirm that it’s
running in a way they expect based on your agreement.
Negative Loan Covenants
Lastly, we have negative loan covenants. Unlike affirmative loan covenants where you’re
expected to do something, with negative covenants you’re expected not to do something.
These restrict certain actions by the borrower unless agreed upon by the lender.
A common covenant here is restrictions on the distributions that can be made to
shareholders such as dividends.
Another could prevent management fees from being paid to relatives, or related parties. For
example, The lender might not want you paying your family from the business while
they’re still owed money.
Another might be buying and selling the company’s assets or even companies without their
permission. If those assets are part of security for the loan, then a covenant will be included
to require you don’t sell those assets.
Many of the restrictions these covenants outline attempt to limit the borrowers ability to
compromise the loan repayment.
Remember, the lenders are always at a distance. To continue doing their job, they need to
know what’s going on at the company, so they can ensure the money is going to be paid
back.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 5 --

And when borrowers go outside of their covenants, the lender has to report them.
Sometimes, if it’s a small breach, you might be able to get the lender to create a waiver that
would allow you to accommodate the issue.
For example, maybe your financial reports were a few weeks late. Of course, that’s a breach
of your loan agreement. But if you go and explain your circumstances, they might get you a
waiver.
But if the breach is serious, they can suspend your loan... Or they can call your loan,
meaning demand early payment. And that’s not a situation you want to be in because called
loans are very challenging.
Lenders can also seize assets used as collateral, end all future borrowing that you might
intend to do with them, and even initiate legal action.
Usually the consequences are far less severe, and the lender will just charge additional fees
or increase rates.
But loan covenants are important. And it’s important that you have them clearly listed out
and understood or you run the risk of breaching them.
You should understand what you’re agreeing to, what’s required of you, and the impact it
could have on your business if you aren’t able to abide by them.
It’s also worth doing this exercise so you can decide what’s reasonable and potentially go
back to the lender to renegotiate the covenants to some extent.
Loan covenants are very important and should be taken seriously. Because they can
seriously derail your company if you don’t keep track of them.
Another note… Never try to hide a covenant breach from the lender. Be upfront about it if it
happens. Or approach the lender if you think you’re in danger of breaching a covenant and
let them know you’re having difficulty maintaining it.
You’ll find that lenders will be more cooperative if you’re honest and upfront. Provide a
reasonable explanation of what’s going on and why.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 5 --

They're likely not going to hold one slip up against you and are likely to remain bullish on
your business.
It’s always better for them to find out ahead of time, from you, than finding out themselves
after the fact.
You can’t avoid covenants but they can be negotiated and they need to be managed.
That covers everything in the “Attracting Financiers” module.
Next, we’re going to jump into perhaps the most exciting part of the course... closing the
deal.
See you in the next module.
